summary: Jeff Atwood examines Moore's Law by comparing its theoretical predictions against real-world CPU performance benchmarks. He notes that transistor counts have actually been doubling every 24 months rather than the commonly cited 18 months. Using SysMark 2004 benchmarks, he demonstrates that it took 26 months for practical PC performance to double, closely tracking the transistor doubling rate. This validates the looser interpretation of Moore's Law as a reliable predictor of computing power increases.
key_points: The popular 18-month formulation of Moore's Law is inaccurate; transistor counts have actually doubled every 24 months
SysMark 2004 benchmarks provide a practical way to measure real-world performance gains calibrated against a reference system
The Pentium 4 EE 3.2 GHz doubled the performance of the P4 2.0 GHz in 26 months, closely matching transistor doubling rates
CPU transistor count serves as a fairly reliable indicator of overall system performance despite other components improving at different rates
money_quotes: The number of transistors on a CPU hasn't actually been doubling every 18 months; it's been doubling every 24 months.
That's why I prefer the second, looser definition of Moore's law: dramatic increases in computing power per unit cost.
It took 26 months to double real world performance in SysMark 2004.
CPU transistor count is fairly reliable indicator of overall performance.
key_insight: Real-world CPU performance doubles approximately every two years, closely tracking transistor count increases and validating Moore's Law as a practical predictor of computing power growth.
